name: English IV - Media & Analytical Writing
nameZh: 英语四（媒体与分析写作）
description: An advanced English course combining media literacy with analytical writing, teaching students to critically read, evaluate, and write about media texts including news, digital content, and visual media.

modules.title: Media Literacy
modules.objectives: Define media literacy and understand why critical evaluation of media messages is essential in the modern world.
modules.readingNote: Read the overview of media literacy principles.
modules.videoNote: Watch the introduction to media literacy.
modules.practiceNote: Browse AllSides to see news stories from multiple political perspectives.
modules.assignment: Choose a news story and compare how three different outlets cover it. Write a one-page analysis of the differences in framing, language, and emphasis.

modules.title: Analyzing News Media
modules.objectives: Apply analytical frameworks to evaluate news articles for accuracy, bias, sourcing, and journalistic standards.
modules.readingNote: Read articles on news analysis and journalistic ethics from Poynter.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on how to analyze news articles.
modules.practiceNote: Use FactCheck.org to investigate a recent news claim.
modules.assignment: Analyze a news article using SIFT (Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims) and write a one-page evaluation.

modules.title: Visual Rhetoric
modules.objectives: Analyze how images, design, color, and layout create meaning and persuade audiences.
modules.readingNote: Read about rhetorical principles applied to visual media.
modules.videoNote: Watch the introduction to visual rhetoric.
modules.practiceNote: Analyze an advertisement's visual rhetoric on Adbusters.
modules.assignment: Choose a print advertisement and write a rhetorical analysis examining how visual elements (image, color, layout, text) work together to persuade the audience.

modules.title: Digital Communication
modules.objectives: Understand how digital media changes communication norms, audience expectations, and message construction.
modules.readingNote: Read a Pew Research report on digital communication trends.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on digital communication and its effects.
modules.practiceNote: Explore digital literacy resources on Common Sense Media.
modules.assignment: Create a short analysis comparing the same message delivered via three different digital platforms (email, Twitter/X, and a blog post), explaining how the medium shapes the message.

modules.title: Investigative Reading
modules.objectives: Apply investigative reading strategies to trace claims, verify sources, and identify misinformation.
modules.readingNote: Read a few fact-check cases on Snopes to understand investigative reading.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on lateral reading and fact-checking.
modules.assignment: Choose a viral social media claim, use lateral reading to investigate its accuracy, and write a one-page fact-check with your findings and evidence.

modules.title: Analytical Essay Writing
modules.objectives: Write well-structured analytical essays about media texts using textual evidence and critical frameworks.
modules.videoNote: Watch the tutorial on analytical essay development.
modules.practiceNote: Complete analytical writing exercises on NoRedInk.
modules.assignment: Write a 4-paragraph analytical essay on a media text of your choice, developing an original thesis about how it uses specific techniques to achieve its purpose.

modules.title: Research in the Digital Age
modules.objectives: Navigate digital information environments effectively, evaluate online sources, and conduct ethical research.
modules.readingNote: Read about online research strategies and source evaluation.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on research in the digital age.
modules.practiceNote: Practice searching academic databases for credible sources.
modules.assignment: Research a media topic using at least four credible digital sources and create an annotated bibliography evaluating each source's credibility and usefulness.

modules.title: Bias & Credibility
modules.objectives: Identify types of media bias, understand how bias influences reporting, and evaluate the credibility of media sources.
modules.readingNote: Study the AllSides Media Bias Chart and read about how bias is identified.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on identifying bias in news media.
modules.practiceNote: Look up several news sources on Media Bias Fact Check.
modules.assignment: Select one news story covered by two outlets with different political leanings and write an essay analyzing how bias affects each outlet's coverage.

modules.title: Opinion & Editorial Writing
modules.objectives: Write clear, persuasive opinion pieces and editorials that present a position supported by evidence and reasoning.
modules.readingNote: Read about persuasive and opinion writing techniques.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on editorial and opinion writing.
modules.practiceNote: Read opinion pieces from the New York Times and note their structure.
modules.assignment: Write a 400-word editorial on a current media or technology issue, taking a clear position and supporting it with at least three pieces of evidence.

modules.title: Multimedia Presentation
modules.objectives: Design and deliver an effective multimedia presentation that combines text, images, and data to communicate an analytical argument.
modules.readingNote: Read about principles of effective presentation design.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on creating effective multimedia presentations.
modules.practiceNote: Create a sample slide deck on Canva using design principles.
modules.assignment: Create a 10-slide multimedia presentation on a media literacy topic, incorporating images, data visualizations, and clear analytical claims.

modules.title: Film Analysis
modules.objectives: Analyze films as texts using cinematic techniques including camera work, editing, sound, and narrative to develop analytical arguments.
modules.readingNote: Read about analyzing visual and filmic texts.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on film analysis techniques.
modules.practiceNote: Read a film essay on the Criterion Collection website.
modules.assignment: Watch a short film or film clip and write a 400-word analysis examining how specific cinematic techniques create meaning and support the film's theme.

modules.title: Social Media & Writing
modules.objectives: Analyze how social media platforms shape writing conventions, and write effectively for digital audiences.
modules.readingNote: Read a Pew Research report on social media usage and its effects.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on writing for social media audiences.
modules.practiceNote: Complete a social media literacy activity on Common Sense Media.
modules.assignment: Write the same analytical argument as a 280-character tweet thread (at least 5 tweets), a LinkedIn post, and a blog post, reflecting on how the platform changes your writing.

modules.title: Publishing & Audience
modules.objectives: Understand the publishing process and how audience awareness shapes writing decisions from draft to final publication.
modules.readingNote: Read about writing for different audiences and publication contexts.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on writing for specific audiences.
modules.practiceNote: Read examples of published essays on Medium to study audience-oriented writing.
modules.assignment: Identify a target publication (school newspaper, blog, online magazine) and write a 500-word piece specifically tailored to that outlet's audience and style guidelines.

modules.title: Capstone Media Project
modules.objectives: Produce an original media analysis or media product that synthesizes course skills in critical reading, analytical writing, and media literacy.
modules.readingNote: Review media literacy frameworks for your capstone project.
modules.videoNote: Watch the overview of media literacy principles for your final project.
modules.practiceNote: Use Canva or another tool to create your final media project.
modules.assignment: Create a 6-8 page analytical research paper or a multimedia project (video essay, podcast, or digital magazine) on a media literacy topic of your choice, incorporating research, analysis, and original argument.

quizQuestions.question: What is media literacy?
options: The ability to access, analyze, evaluate, and create media in various forms
The ability to use social media platforms
quizQuestions.answer: The ability to access, analyze, evaluate, and create media in various forms
quizQuestions.explanation: Media literacy is the ability to critically access, analyze, evaluate, and create media messages in all forms.

quizQuestions.question: Why is media literacy important in modern society?
options: People are constantly exposed to media messages that shape their beliefs and decisions
It helps you use technology faster
It replaces the need for reading books
quizQuestions.answer: People are constantly exposed to media messages that shape their beliefs and decisions
quizQuestions.explanation: Media literacy helps individuals critically evaluate the messages they consume, making them more informed citizens.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'framing' mean in media analysis?
options: The physical frame around a newspaper
How a media outlet selects and presents information to shape how audiences perceive an issue
The format of a television show
quizQuestions.answer: How a media outlet selects and presents information to shape how audiences perceive an issue
quizQuestions.explanation: Framing refers to how media choose which aspects of a story to emphasize, influencing how audiences interpret events.

quizQuestions.question: What is the difference between news and opinion content in media?
options: News reports verifiable facts; opinion expresses the writer's views and interpretations
There is no difference in modern media
quizQuestions.answer: News reports verifiable facts; opinion expresses the writer's views and interpretations
quizQuestions.explanation: News journalism aims to report facts objectively; opinion pieces explicitly express the author's perspective.

quizQuestions.question: What is convergence in media?
options: The blending of different media platforms and technologies into a single digital environment
quizQuestions.answer: The blending of different media platforms and technologies into a single digital environment
quizQuestions.explanation: Convergence refers to how different forms of media—print, video, audio—now coexist and blend on digital platforms.

quizQuestions.question: What is the SIFT method for evaluating news?
options: Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims
quizQuestions.answer: Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims
quizQuestions.explanation: SIFT is a fact-checking strategy: Stop before sharing, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, and Trace claims to original context.

quizQuestions.question: What is 'lateral reading' in source verification?
options: Reading a source from left to right
Opening multiple browser tabs to research what others say about a source, rather than reading deeply within it
quizQuestions.answer: Opening multiple browser tabs to research what others say about a source, rather than reading deeply within it
quizQuestions.explanation: Lateral reading means checking what other credible sources say about a website or author rather than evaluating the site on its own terms.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of fact-checking?
options: To verify whether claims made in media content are accurate and supported by evidence
quizQuestions.answer: To verify whether claims made in media content are accurate and supported by evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: Fact-checking is the process of investigating and verifying factual claims made in news or other content.

quizQuestions.question: What distinguishes a credible news source?
options: It has a clear editorial process, transparency about sources, and a record of accuracy
quizQuestions.answer: It has a clear editorial process, transparency about sources, and a record of accuracy
quizQuestions.explanation: Credible news sources are transparent about their methods, cite sources, issue corrections, and have a consistent record of accuracy.

quizQuestions.question: What is 'clickbait'?
options: Misleading or sensationalized content designed to attract clicks rather than inform
quizQuestions.answer: Misleading or sensationalized content designed to attract clicks rather than inform
quizQuestions.explanation: Clickbait uses exaggerated or misleading headlines to generate traffic without delivering meaningful or accurate information.

quizQuestions.question: What is visual rhetoric?
options: The use of visual elements to communicate meaning and persuade an audience
quizQuestions.answer: The use of visual elements to communicate meaning and persuade an audience
quizQuestions.explanation: Visual rhetoric examines how images, design, color, and layout function as forms of communication and persuasion.

quizQuestions.question: How does color function in visual rhetoric?
options: Color conveys emotion, establishes tone, and directs the viewer's attention
It indicates the age of the image
quizQuestions.answer: Color conveys emotion, establishes tone, and directs the viewer's attention
quizQuestions.explanation: Color is a powerful visual tool that creates emotional responses and guides the viewer's interpretation of an image.

quizQuestions.question: What is 'composition' in visual analysis?
options: The written text in an image
The arrangement of visual elements within the frame
quizQuestions.answer: The arrangement of visual elements within the frame
quizQuestions.explanation: Composition refers to how visual elements are arranged within the image, affecting emphasis, balance, and meaning.

quizQuestions.question: What makes an image 'iconic' in media studies?
options: It comes to symbolize a broader event, idea, or cultural moment beyond its literal content
quizQuestions.answer: It comes to symbolize a broader event, idea, or cultural moment beyond its literal content
quizQuestions.explanation: An iconic image transcends its literal content to become a powerful cultural symbol representing larger ideas or events.

quizQuestions.question: What is the relationship between text and image in multimodal media?
options: Text and image always mean the same thing
Text and image interact to create meaning that neither can produce alone
Images are always more important than text
Text explains what images cannot show
quizQuestions.answer: Text and image interact to create meaning that neither can produce alone
quizQuestions.explanation: In multimodal media, text and images work together dynamically, each modifying and enhancing the meaning of the other.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'the medium is the message' mean?
options: The content of a message is more important than how it is delivered
The form or channel through which a message is delivered shapes its meaning and impact
Messages in print are more credible than digital messages
Media platforms all work the same way
quizQuestions.answer: The form or channel through which a message is delivered shapes its meaning and impact
quizQuestions.explanation: Marshall McLuhan's phrase means that the medium itself—TV, internet, print—fundamentally shapes how a message is received and understood.

quizQuestions.question: How does digital communication differ from print communication?
options: Digital communication is immediate, interactive, and allows for rapid distribution and response
quizQuestions.answer: Digital communication is immediate, interactive, and allows for rapid distribution and response
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital media enables instant, interactive communication with global audiences, fundamentally changing how messages are created and received.

quizQuestions.question: What is a 'filter bubble' in digital communication?
options: An algorithmically created environment where users are exposed only to content that aligns with their existing views
quizQuestions.answer: An algorithmically created environment where users are exposed only to content that aligns with their existing views
quizQuestions.explanation: Filter bubbles occur when algorithms show users only content that matches their existing preferences, limiting exposure to diverse perspectives.

quizQuestions.question: What is viral content?
options: Content that spreads rapidly and widely through sharing on digital networks
quizQuestions.answer: Content that spreads rapidly and widely through sharing on digital networks
quizQuestions.explanation: Viral content spreads rapidly through social sharing, often because it provokes strong emotional responses.

quizQuestions.question: What is digital citizenship?
options: The responsible and ethical use of digital technology and online communities
quizQuestions.answer: The responsible and ethical use of digital technology and online communities
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital citizenship involves using technology ethically, respectfully, and safely while contributing positively to online communities.

quizQuestions.question: What is misinformation?
options: False or inaccurate information that is spread without malicious intent
quizQuestions.answer: False or inaccurate information that is spread without malicious intent
quizQuestions.explanation: Misinformation is false or inaccurate information shared by people who may not know it is incorrect.

quizQuestions.question: What is disinformation?
options: False information spread deliberately to deceive or mislead
Information that is hard to understand
quizQuestions.answer: False information spread deliberately to deceive or mislead
quizQuestions.explanation: Disinformation is intentionally false or misleading information created and spread to deceive audiences.

quizQuestions.question: What does it mean to trace a claim to its original source?
options: To find the original creator, context, and evidence behind a claim rather than relying on secondary reports
To read the most popular version of a story
quizQuestions.answer: To find the original creator, context, and evidence behind a claim rather than relying on secondary reports
quizQuestions.explanation: Tracing a claim means going back to the primary source of a claim to verify whether it has been accurately reported.

quizQuestions.question: What is confirmation bias in media consumption?
options: The tendency to seek out and favor information that confirms existing beliefs
quizQuestions.answer: The tendency to seek out and favor information that confirms existing beliefs
quizQuestions.explanation: Confirmation bias leads people to accept information that agrees with their existing views and reject information that challenges them.

quizQuestions.question: What is 'deep fake' media?
options: AI-generated video or audio that realistically depicts people saying or doing things they never said or did
quizQuestions.answer: AI-generated video or audio that realistically depicts people saying or doing things they never said or did
quizQuestions.explanation: Deep fakes are synthetic media created using artificial intelligence to convincingly depict real people in fabricated scenarios.

quizQuestions.question: What is an analytical thesis in a media essay?
options: A summary of the media text
A debatable claim about how or why a media text works to achieve a particular effect
A description of the media text
A list of the media text's features
quizQuestions.answer: A debatable claim about how or why a media text works to achieve a particular effect
quizQuestions.explanation: An analytical thesis makes a specific, arguable claim about the meaning, strategy, or effect of a media text.

quizQuestions.question: What is textual evidence in media analysis?
options: Specific details from the media text that support the analytical claim
A summary of the media text
A citation from a news article
quizQuestions.answer: Specific details from the media text that support the analytical claim
quizQuestions.explanation: Textual evidence in media analysis refers to specific details—words, images, data—from the media text that support the writer's argument.

quizQuestions.question: What should follow evidence in an analytical media essay?
options: Analysis explaining how the evidence supports the claim
quizQuestions.answer: Analysis explaining how the evidence supports the claim
quizQuestions.explanation: After presenting evidence, the writer must explain how it connects to and supports the analytical argument.

quizQuestions.question: What is a media text?
options: Any form of communication produced and distributed by media, including articles, films, advertisements, and social media posts
quizQuestions.answer: Any form of communication produced and distributed by media, including articles, films, advertisements, and social media posts
quizQuestions.explanation: A media text is any artifact created by media organizations or individuals—written, visual, audio, or digital.

quizQuestions.question: What distinguishes media analysis from media summary?
options: Media analysis evaluates how and why a text works; summary only describes what it contains
quizQuestions.answer: Media analysis evaluates how and why a text works; summary only describes what it contains
quizQuestions.explanation: Analysis examines the techniques, strategies, and effects of a media text; summary merely describes its content.

quizQuestions.question: What is a database in academic research?
options: A collection of social media posts
An organized collection of peer-reviewed articles, books, and academic resources
quizQuestions.answer: An organized collection of peer-reviewed articles, books, and academic resources
quizQuestions.explanation: Academic databases provide access to scholarly journals, peer-reviewed articles, and research publications.

quizQuestions.question: What is the difference between a search engine and an academic database?
options: A search engine indexes all web content; a database indexes curated scholarly sources
Academic databases are free to use
Search engines provide more accurate results
quizQuestions.answer: A search engine indexes all web content; a database indexes curated scholarly sources
quizQuestions.explanation: Search engines cast a wide net over all web content; academic databases focus on vetted scholarly sources.

quizQuestions.question: What does it mean to evaluate a website's 'authority'?
options: Checking how many visitors it has
Assessing the credentials and expertise of the author or organization behind the content
Finding out how old the website is
quizQuestions.answer: Assessing the credentials and expertise of the author or organization behind the content
quizQuestions.explanation: Evaluating authority means determining whether the author or organization has the expertise and credibility to speak on the topic.

quizQuestions.question: What is an open-access journal?
options: A journal anyone can write for
A peer-reviewed journal whose content is freely available online to all readers
quizQuestions.answer: A peer-reviewed journal whose content is freely available online to all readers
quizQuestions.explanation: Open-access journals are peer-reviewed publications that make their content freely available without subscription fees.

quizQuestions.question: Why is it important to use a variety of source types in media research?
options: To triangulate findings across different types of evidence, strengthening the argument's credibility
Because one type of source is never accurate
quizQuestions.answer: To triangulate findings across different types of evidence, strengthening the argument's credibility
quizQuestions.explanation: Using diverse source types—news articles, academic studies, primary documents—provides multiple perspectives and strengthens research.

quizQuestions.question: What is media bias?
options: When media coverage systematically favors certain perspectives, groups, or interpretations over others
quizQuestions.answer: When media coverage systematically favors certain perspectives, groups, or interpretations over others
quizQuestions.explanation: Media bias occurs when reporting reflects a systematic preference for certain viewpoints, whether in story selection, framing, or language.

quizQuestions.question: What is 'false balance' in journalism?
options: When a news article has too many quotations
Giving equal coverage to positions that are not equally supported by evidence, creating a false impression of debate
When a story has no sources
quizQuestions.answer: Giving equal coverage to positions that are not equally supported by evidence, creating a false impression of debate
quizQuestions.explanation: False balance treats two sides as equally credible when the evidence strongly favors one position, misleading audiences.

quizQuestions.question: What is agenda-setting theory?
options: The theory that media don't tell us what to think, but tell us what to think about by selecting which stories to cover
quizQuestions.answer: The theory that media don't tell us what to think, but tell us what to think about by selecting which stories to cover
quizQuestions.explanation: Agenda-setting theory suggests media influence public attention by deciding which stories and issues to cover prominently.

quizQuestions.question: What is selection bias in news reporting?
options: When media systematically choose which stories to cover in ways that favor particular viewpoints or narratives
quizQuestions.answer: When media systematically choose which stories to cover in ways that favor particular viewpoints or narratives
quizQuestions.explanation: Selection bias involves systematically choosing or avoiding certain stories based on viewpoint, creating a skewed picture of reality.

quizQuestions.question: Why should media consumers seek multiple sources of information?
options: To get a more complete and balanced picture by comparing coverage across different outlets
Because one source is always wrong
quizQuestions.answer: To get a more complete and balanced picture by comparing coverage across different outlets
quizQuestions.explanation: Consulting multiple sources helps identify bias, fill in missing perspectives, and form more accurate understanding.

quizQuestions.question: What is the main goal of an editorial?
options: To express and argue for a specific opinion or position on an issue
To entertain readers with a story
quizQuestions.answer: To express and argue for a specific opinion or position on an issue
quizQuestions.explanation: An editorial presents and argues for a specific viewpoint, aiming to persuade readers.

quizQuestions.question: What is the difference between an editorial and a news article?
options: A news article reports facts; an editorial expresses and argues a viewpoint
quizQuestions.answer: A news article reports facts; an editorial expresses and argues a viewpoint
quizQuestions.explanation: News articles aim to report facts objectively; editorials explicitly advocate for a position.

quizQuestions.question: What should an opinion editorial include?
options: A clear position, supporting evidence and reasoning, and acknowledgment of counterarguments
quizQuestions.answer: A clear position, supporting evidence and reasoning, and acknowledgment of counterarguments
quizQuestions.explanation: Strong editorials present a clear claim, support it with evidence and logic, and address opposing viewpoints.

quizQuestions.question: What is a 'call to action' in persuasive writing?
options: A direct request for the audience to take a specific action
quizQuestions.answer: A direct request for the audience to take a specific action
quizQuestions.explanation: A call to action explicitly asks the audience to do something in response to the argument.

quizQuestions.question: Why is knowing your audience important when writing an editorial?
options: To tailor your argument, evidence, and tone to what will be most persuasive to your specific readers
quizQuestions.answer: To tailor your argument, evidence, and tone to what will be most persuasive to your specific readers
quizQuestions.explanation: Audience awareness helps writers choose evidence, examples, and language that will resonate with and persuade their intended readers.

quizQuestions.question: What is a multimedia presentation?
options: A presentation that combines text, images, audio, video, and/or data visualization to communicate a message
quizQuestions.answer: A presentation that combines text, images, audio, video, and/or data visualization to communicate a message
quizQuestions.explanation: Multimedia presentations integrate multiple modalities to communicate effectively to audiences.

quizQuestions.question: What is the 'less is more' principle in presentation design?
options: Minimizing text and visual clutter on each slide to maximize clarity and impact
Having fewer ideas in a presentation
quizQuestions.answer: Minimizing text and visual clutter on each slide to maximize clarity and impact
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective presentation design uses minimal text and focused visuals, letting key ideas stand out clearly.

quizQuestions.question: What is data visualization?
options: A graphic representation of information (charts, graphs, infographics) to make data understandable
quizQuestions.answer: A graphic representation of information (charts, graphs, infographics) to make data understandable
quizQuestions.explanation: Data visualization transforms complex data into visual formats that are easier to understand and communicate.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of a presentation narrative?
options: To read text aloud from slides
To guide the audience through the argument with a clear story or logical sequence
quizQuestions.answer: To guide the audience through the argument with a clear story or logical sequence
quizQuestions.explanation: A presentation narrative creates a coherent flow that guides the audience through the argument in a logical and engaging way.

quizQuestions.question: What is the role of design in a multimedia presentation?
options: To make the presentation look professional only
To support and enhance the communication of the argument through visual clarity and coherence
To hide the lack of content
quizQuestions.answer: To support and enhance the communication of the argument through visual clarity and coherence
quizQuestions.explanation: Design choices—layout, color, typography—should serve the argument, making content clear and engaging.

quizQuestions.question: What is mise-en-scène in film analysis?
options: A French term for the plot
Everything placed in front of the camera, including setting, lighting, costume, and actors' positions
The editing style of a film
quizQuestions.answer: Everything placed in front of the camera, including setting, lighting, costume, and actors' positions
quizQuestions.explanation: Mise-en-scène refers to the visual elements arranged within the frame: setting, props, lighting, and staging.

quizQuestions.question: What does cinematography refer to in film analysis?
options: The art and technique of camera work, including framing, movement, and lighting
quizQuestions.answer: The art and technique of camera work, including framing, movement, and lighting
quizQuestions.explanation: Cinematography encompasses all decisions about how the camera captures images, including angles, movement, and lighting.

quizQuestions.question: What is editing in film and why is it significant?
options: The joining of shots to create sequence, rhythm, and meaning
quizQuestions.answer: The joining of shots to create sequence, rhythm, and meaning
quizQuestions.explanation: Editing assembles shots to create narrative flow, control pacing, juxtapose images, and shape the audience's experience.

quizQuestions.question: What is a close-up shot in filmmaking?
options: A shot taken from far away
A shot that frames a face or object in detail, drawing attention to expression or significance
A shot that shows the whole scene
quizQuestions.answer: A shot that frames a face or object in detail, drawing attention to expression or significance
quizQuestions.explanation: Close-ups focus tightly on a face or detail to emphasize emotion, importance, or meaning.

quizQuestions.question: What is diegetic sound in film?
options: Sound that exists within the world of the film and can be heard by the characters
quizQuestions.answer: Sound that exists within the world of the film and can be heard by the characters
quizQuestions.explanation: Diegetic sound originates from the story world—dialogue, footsteps, ambient sounds that characters can hear.

quizQuestions.question: How do social media platforms shape writing style?
options: They have no effect on writing
Platform constraints—character limits, audience expectations, and algorithmic preferences—shape how content is written
quizQuestions.answer: Platform constraints—character limits, audience expectations, and algorithmic preferences—shape how content is written
quizQuestions.explanation: Each social media platform has unique constraints and audience expectations that significantly influence how content is written and received.

quizQuestions.question: What is engagement in social media writing?
options: The number of words in a post
Interactions (likes, shares, comments, clicks) that indicate an audience's response to content
quizQuestions.answer: Interactions (likes, shares, comments, clicks) that indicate an audience's response to content
quizQuestions.explanation: Engagement refers to how actively the audience responds to content through various platform interactions.

quizQuestions.question: What is a hashtag and how is it used rhetorically?
options: A keyword marked with # that categorizes content and can create community or amplify a message
quizQuestions.answer: A keyword marked with # that categorizes content and can create community or amplify a message
quizQuestions.explanation: Hashtags organize content around topics and can be used to join conversations, build movements, or increase visibility.

quizQuestions.question: What is influencer marketing in social media?
options: Using individuals with large social media followings to promote products, ideas, or brands
quizQuestions.answer: Using individuals with large social media followings to promote products, ideas, or brands
quizQuestions.explanation: Influencer marketing leverages trusted social media personalities to reach and persuade their established audiences.

quizQuestions.question: What ethical considerations arise in social media writing?
options: Accuracy, privacy, attribution, and the potential for spreading misinformation or causing harm
quizQuestions.answer: Accuracy, privacy, attribution, and the potential for spreading misinformation or causing harm
quizQuestions.explanation: Social media writing carries ethical responsibilities around accuracy, respect for privacy, proper attribution, and awareness of potential harm.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'publishing for a specific audience' mean?
options: Tailoring the content, tone, and format of writing to the expectations and needs of a particular readership
quizQuestions.answer: Tailoring the content, tone, and format of writing to the expectations and needs of a particular readership
quizQuestions.explanation: Publishing for a specific audience means understanding and addressing the knowledge, values, and expectations of your intended readers.

quizQuestions.question: What is editorial voice?
options: The distinctive tone, perspective, and style that identifies a publication or writer
The use of formal language only
quizQuestions.answer: The distinctive tone, perspective, and style that identifies a publication or writer
quizQuestions.explanation: Editorial voice is the recognizable personality and perspective that distinguishes a publication's or writer's work.

quizQuestions.question: What is a style guide in publishing?
options: A set of standards for writing and formatting used consistently within a publication
quizQuestions.answer: A set of standards for writing and formatting used consistently within a publication
quizQuestions.explanation: A style guide establishes rules for grammar, punctuation, formatting, and terminology to ensure consistency across a publication.

quizQuestions.question: What is the revision process in professional writing?
options: Multiple rounds of editing for content, style, and mechanics before publication
Having one person read the draft
quizQuestions.answer: Multiple rounds of editing for content, style, and mechanics before publication
quizQuestions.explanation: Professional writing involves systematic revision at multiple levels—content, structure, style, and proofreading—before publication.

quizQuestions.question: Why is attribution important in published writing?
options: It gives credit to sources, builds credibility, and allows readers to verify information
It replaces the need for original ideas
quizQuestions.answer: It gives credit to sources, builds credibility, and allows readers to verify information
quizQuestions.explanation: Attribution acknowledges source material, demonstrates intellectual honesty, and enables readers to check facts and find further information.

quizQuestions.question: What is a capstone project in media analysis?
options: The first assignment in a course
A culminating project that synthesizes course learning in a substantial original work
quizQuestions.answer: A culminating project that synthesizes course learning in a substantial original work
quizQuestions.explanation: A capstone project integrates the skills and knowledge developed throughout a course into a major original work.

quizQuestions.question: What is a video essay?
options: A written essay about video games
An analytical argument delivered in video format, combining narration, visuals, and editing
quizQuestions.answer: An analytical argument delivered in video format, combining narration, visuals, and editing
quizQuestions.explanation: A video essay presents analytical arguments through the medium of video, integrating narration, images, film clips, and editing.

quizQuestions.question: What distinguishes a media analysis from a summary of media content?
options: Analysis evaluates how and why a media text works; summary describes what it contains
quizQuestions.answer: Analysis evaluates how and why a media text works; summary describes what it contains
quizQuestions.explanation: Analysis examines techniques, strategies, and effects; summary merely describes content without evaluating how it functions.

quizQuestions.question: What is a digital magazine?
options: An online version of a print magazine only
A curated digital publication featuring multimedia content, articles, and visual design
quizQuestions.answer: A curated digital publication featuring multimedia content, articles, and visual design
quizQuestions.explanation: A digital magazine is a curated online publication that integrates text, images, video, and interactive elements.

quizQuestions.question: Why is media literacy a lifelong skill?
options: Because you can only learn it in school
Because the media landscape continuously evolves and critical evaluation skills are always needed
quizQuestions.answer: Because the media landscape continuously evolves and critical evaluation skills are always needed
quizQuestions.explanation: As media forms, platforms, and technologies change, the ability to critically evaluate media messages remains essential.

questions.question: What is media literacy?
options: The ability to access, analyze, evaluate, and create media messages
questions.answer: The ability to access, analyze, evaluate, and create media messages
questions.explanation: Media literacy is the ability to critically access, analyze, evaluate, and create media in various forms.

questions.question: What does 'framing' mean in media analysis?
options: The physical border around a screen
How media selects and presents information to shape audience perception
questions.answer: How media selects and presents information to shape audience perception
questions.explanation: Framing refers to how media choose which aspects of a story to emphasize, influencing interpretation.

questions.question: What is the SIFT method?
options: Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims
questions.answer: Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims
questions.explanation: SIFT is a fact-checking strategy for evaluating online information.

questions.question: What is visual rhetoric?
options: The use of visual elements to communicate meaning and persuade an audience
questions.answer: The use of visual elements to communicate meaning and persuade an audience
questions.explanation: Visual rhetoric examines how images, design, color, and layout function as persuasion.

questions.question: What does 'the medium is the message' mean?
options: Content is more important than delivery method
The channel through which a message is delivered shapes its meaning and impact
questions.answer: The channel through which a message is delivered shapes its meaning and impact
questions.explanation: McLuhan's phrase means that the medium itself fundamentally shapes how a message is received.

questions.question: What is misinformation?
options: False or inaccurate information spread without malicious intent
questions.answer: False or inaccurate information spread without malicious intent
questions.explanation: Misinformation is false or inaccurate information shared by people who may not know it is incorrect.

questions.question: What distinguishes media analysis from media summary?
options: Analysis evaluates how and why a text works; summary only describes what it contains
questions.answer: Analysis evaluates how and why a text works; summary only describes what it contains
questions.explanation: Analysis examines techniques, strategies, and effects; summary describes content without evaluating how it functions.

questions.question: What is media bias?
options: When media coverage systematically favors certain perspectives over others
questions.answer: When media coverage systematically favors certain perspectives over others
questions.explanation: Media bias is a systematic preference for certain viewpoints in story selection, framing, or language.

questions.question: What is the main goal of an editorial?
options: To express and argue for a specific opinion on an issue
questions.answer: To express and argue for a specific opinion on an issue
questions.explanation: An editorial presents and argues for a specific viewpoint, aiming to persuade readers.

questions.question: What is a filter bubble?
options: An environment where users see only content that aligns with their existing views
questions.answer: An environment where users see only content that aligns with their existing views
questions.explanation: Filter bubbles occur when algorithms show users only content matching their existing preferences.

questions.question: What is mise-en-scène in film analysis?
options: Everything placed in front of the camera including setting, lighting, and staging
questions.answer: Everything placed in front of the camera including setting, lighting, and staging
questions.explanation: Mise-en-scène refers to the visual elements arranged within the frame.

questions.question: What is confirmation bias?
options: The tendency to favor information that confirms existing beliefs
questions.answer: The tendency to favor information that confirms existing beliefs
questions.explanation: Confirmation bias leads people to accept information that agrees with their existing views.

questions.question: What is agenda-setting theory?
options: The theory that media tell us what to think about by selecting which stories to cover
questions.answer: The theory that media tell us what to think about by selecting which stories to cover
questions.explanation: Agenda-setting theory suggests media influence public attention through story selection.

questions.question: What does 'lateral reading' involve?
options: Checking what other credible sources say about a source rather than reading deeply within it
questions.answer: Checking what other credible sources say about a source rather than reading deeply within it
questions.explanation: Lateral reading means checking what other sources say about a website or author rather than evaluating the site on its own terms.

questions.question: What is disinformation?
options: Deliberately false information created and spread to deceive
Information that is hard to understand
questions.answer: Deliberately false information created and spread to deceive
questions.explanation: Disinformation is intentionally false or misleading information created to deceive audiences.

questions.question: What is a media text?
options: Any form of communication produced by media, including articles, films, advertisements, and social media
questions.answer: Any form of communication produced by media, including articles, films, advertisements, and social media
questions.explanation: A media text is any artifact created by media—written, visual, audio, or digital.

questions.question: What is cinematography?
options: The art and technique of camera work including framing, movement, and lighting
questions.answer: The art and technique of camera work including framing, movement, and lighting
questions.explanation: Cinematography encompasses all decisions about how the camera captures images.

questions.question: What is a capstone media project?
options: The first assignment in a course
A culminating project that synthesizes course learning in a substantial original work
questions.answer: A culminating project that synthesizes course learning in a substantial original work
questions.explanation: A capstone project integrates the skills developed throughout a course into a major original work.

questions.question: What is 'false balance' in journalism?
options: Too many quotations in an article
Giving equal coverage to positions not equally supported by evidence
questions.answer: Giving equal coverage to positions not equally supported by evidence
questions.explanation: False balance treats two sides as equally credible when evidence strongly favors one position.

questions.question: What is data visualization?
options: A graphic representation of information to make data understandable
questions.answer: A graphic representation of information to make data understandable
questions.explanation: Data visualization transforms complex data into visual formats that are easier to understand.

questions.question: What is a hashtag's rhetorical function?
options: To categorize content and create community or amplify a message
questions.answer: To categorize content and create community or amplify a message
questions.explanation: Hashtags organize content around topics and can build movements or increase visibility.

questions.question: What is selection bias in news reporting?
options: Systematically choosing to cover certain stories in ways that favor particular viewpoints
questions.answer: Systematically choosing to cover certain stories in ways that favor particular viewpoints
questions.explanation: Selection bias involves systematically choosing or avoiding stories based on viewpoint, creating a skewed picture of reality.

questions.question: What is a deep fake?
options: AI-generated video or audio depicting people saying or doing things they never did
questions.answer: AI-generated video or audio depicting people saying or doing things they never did
questions.explanation: Deep fakes are synthetic media created using artificial intelligence to convincingly depict fabricated scenarios.

questions.question: What is editorial voice?
options: The distinctive tone and style that identifies a publication or writer
questions.answer: The distinctive tone and style that identifies a publication or writer
questions.explanation: Editorial voice is the recognizable personality and perspective that distinguishes a writer's or publication's work.

questions.question: What is diegetic sound in film?
options: Sound that exists within the world of the film and can be heard by characters
questions.answer: Sound that exists within the world of the film and can be heard by characters
questions.explanation: Diegetic sound originates from the story world—dialogue, footsteps, ambient sounds that characters can hear.

questions.question: What is digital citizenship?
options: The responsible and ethical use of digital technology and online communities
questions.answer: The responsible and ethical use of digital technology and online communities
questions.explanation: Digital citizenship involves using technology ethically, respectfully, and safely.

questions.question: What is a video essay?
options: A written essay about video games
An analytical argument delivered in video format combining narration and visuals
questions.answer: An analytical argument delivered in video format combining narration and visuals
questions.explanation: A video essay presents analytical arguments through video, integrating narration, images, and editing.

questions.question: Why is attribution important in published writing?
options: It credits sources, builds credibility, and allows readers to verify information
questions.answer: It credits sources, builds credibility, and allows readers to verify information
questions.explanation: Attribution acknowledges source material, demonstrates intellectual honesty, and enables fact verification.

questions.question: What does composition mean in visual analysis?
options: The written text in an image
The arrangement of visual elements within the frame
questions.answer: The arrangement of visual elements within the frame
questions.explanation: Composition refers to how visual elements are arranged within the image, affecting emphasis and meaning.

questions.question: Why is media literacy considered a lifelong skill?
options: You can only learn it in school
The media landscape continuously evolves and critical evaluation is always needed
questions.answer: The media landscape continuously evolves and critical evaluation is always needed
questions.explanation: As media forms and technologies change, the ability to critically evaluate media messages remains essential.

questions.question: What is influencer marketing?
options: Using social media personalities to promote products or ideas to their audiences
questions.answer: Using social media personalities to promote products or ideas to their audiences
questions.explanation: Influencer marketing leverages trusted social media personalities to reach and persuade their established audiences.

questions.question: What is a call to action in persuasive writing?
options: A direct request for the audience to take a specific action
questions.answer: A direct request for the audience to take a specific action
questions.explanation: A call to action explicitly asks the audience to do something in response to the argument.

questions.question: What is the 'less is more' principle in presentation design?
options: Minimizing text and visual clutter to maximize clarity and impact
questions.answer: Minimizing text and visual clutter to maximize clarity and impact
questions.explanation: Effective presentation design uses minimal text and focused visuals so key ideas stand out clearly.

questions.question: What makes an image 'iconic' in media studies?
options: It comes to symbolize a broader event, idea, or cultural moment
questions.answer: It comes to symbolize a broader event, idea, or cultural moment
questions.explanation: An iconic image transcends its literal content to become a powerful cultural symbol.

questions.question: What distinguishes a media analysis thesis from a summary statement?
options: An analysis thesis makes a debatable claim about how a media text works; a summary statement describes content
questions.answer: An analysis thesis makes a debatable claim about how a media text works; a summary statement describes content
questions.explanation: An analytical thesis makes an arguable claim about strategy and effect; a summary merely describes what the text contains.
